coredata.dc:title: Enhancing the cavitation erosion resistance of D8m-Ta5Si3 nanocrystalline coatings through Al alloying
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, Jiang
Liu, Wei
Jiang, Shuyun
Munroe, Paul
Xie, Zong-Han
coredata.dc:description: To investigate the effects of Al alloying on the erosion-corrosion resistance of β-Ta5Si3, both a β-Ta5Si3 coating and an Al-alloyed β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating were synthesized on a 316 substrate by the double cathode glow discharge technique. The phase constitution, composition and microstructure of the two coatings were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The two coatings were composed of nearly rounded D8m-β-Ta5Si3 grains with an average size of ∼4 nm, and after the addition of Al, the preferred growth orientation for the β-Ta5Si3 coating changed from (4 0 0) to (0 0 2). The hardness, elastic modulus and contact damage resistance of the coatings were measured using a nanoindentation tester. The results showed that Al alloying improved the contact damage resistance of β-Ta5Si3 with only a slight decrease in hardness. The erosion-corrosion behavior of the two coatings was performed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing a 12 wt% concentration of silica sand under two phase slurry flow condition and in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under ultrasonic cavitation erosion conditions. This revealed that the Al alloyed β-Ta5Si3 has a higher resistance to both erosion-corrosion and ultrasonic cavitation erosion as compared to the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Refractory metal silicides
Erosion–corrosion
Ultrasonic cavitation erosion
Potentiodynamic polarization
EIS

xocs:cp-license-lines.xocs:cp-license-line: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD patterns for β-Ta5Si3 and the Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coatings; cross-sectional SEM images with corresponding EDS spectra for (b) β-Ta5Si3 and (c) Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coatings.

(a) A typical cross-sectional bright-field TEM image taken from outer region of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating and (b) a high-magnification image of the selected region in (a); (c) a plan-view TEM bright-field image of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating, with a corresponding selected area diffraction (SAD) pattern; (d) a high-magnification image of the selected area in (c); (e) a high-resolution electron microscope (HRTEM) image of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating.

(a) A typical cross-sectional bright-field TEM image obtained from the coating/substrate interface of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating; (b) a high-magnification image of the selected area marked in (a).
Load-displacement (p-h) curves for β-Ta5Si3, β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 and 316L SS.

Images of the indentation impressions in the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings subjected to a load of 500 mN with a 5 μm radius spherical indenter. Plan view images: (a) β-Ta5Si3 (b) β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3; cross-sectional SEM images: (c) β-Ta5Si3, (d) β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3; cross-sectional TEM images: (e) β-Ta5Si3, (f) β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3.
Flow velocities of the tested samples as a function of exposure time.
The current responses for the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and 316L SS at different flow velocities in a NaCl solution without (a), and with (b), sand particles at a potential of +0.2.
Polarization curves for (a) uncoated 316L SS, (b) β-Ta5Si3 coating and (c) β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating at different flow velocities in liquid–solid slurry flow.
EIS curves of the tested samples in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution after different times of slurry impingement: (a),(b) the β-Ta5Si3 coating; (c),(d) β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating and (e),(f) 316L SS.

Electronic equivalent circuit (EEC) used in the fitting procedure: (a) 316L SS in static state; (b) β-Ta5Si3 coating and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating in static state; (c) tested samples after impingement.

(a) Total weight loss rate, pure erosion rate, pure corrosion rate and synergistic weight loss rate of the tested samples and (b) percent of synergistic weight loss rate in the total weight loss rate for the three tested samples in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 12 wt% sand particles.

Typical SEM images of the eroded surfaces for the tested samples before and after 20 h of exposure to liquid–solid two phase slurry flow at a flow velocity of 3.85 m/s. (a) and (b) 316L stainless steel, (c) and (d) the β-Ta5Si3 coating, (e) and (f) the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating.

(a) the electrical input powers as a function of exposure time; (b) the open circuit potential (OCP)–time curves for samples tested in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with different input power; (c) the current densities of the tested samples as a function of the electrical input powers at a constant potential of +0.2 VSCE in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.
The potentiodynamic polarization curves for (a) the β-Ta5Si3 coating, (b) the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating and (c) 316L SS in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at different electrical input powers.

Nyquist and Bode plots collected from the tested coatings and the 316L SS in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution after different intervals of ultrasonic cavitation erosion with the input power of 800 W.the β-Ta5Si3 coating ((a), (b)), β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating ((c), (d)) and 316L stainless steel ((e), (f)).
The weight loss as a function of exposure time for the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and the reference 316L SS substrate at an input power of 800 W in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

The eroded surface morphologies of the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and the reference 316L SS after 12 h of cavitation erosion-corrosion tests at an input power of 800 W in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. (a) 316L SS; (b) the β-Ta5Si3 coating; (c) the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating.
The hardness and Young’s modulus for the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and 316L SS.
Polarization parameters of the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and uncoated 316L SS under slurry impingement at various flow velocities.
Electrochemical parameters obtained from numerical fitting for the two coatings and uncoated 316L SS after different times of slurry impingement at a flow velocity of 3.85 m/s.
Polarization parameters of the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and uncoated 316L SS under cavitation testing at different electrical input powers.

Electrochemical parameters obtained from numerical fitting for the β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coatings and uncoated 316L SS after different exposure times of cavitation erosion at a input power of 800 W.

head.ce:title: Enhancing the cavitation erosion resistance of D8m-Ta5Si3 nanocrystalline coatings through Al alloying

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Material Science and Engineering, Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics, 29 Yudao Street, Nanjing 210016, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Material Science and Engineering
Nanjing University of Aeronautics and Astronautics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical & Electrical Engineering, Wuhan Institute of Technology,693 Xiongchu Avenue, Wuhan 430073, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Southeast University, 2 Si Pai Lou, Nanjing 210096, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Materials Science and Engineering, University of New South Wales, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Mechanical Engineering, University of Adelaide, SA 5005, Australia

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: To investigate the effects of Al alloying on the erosion-corrosion resistance of β-Ta5Si3, both a β-Ta5Si3 coating and an Al-alloyed β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating were synthesized on a 316 substrate by the double cathode glow discharge technique. The phase constitution, composition and microstructure of the two coatings were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD), scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The two coatings were composed of nearly rounded D8m-β-Ta5Si3 grains with an average size of ∼4 nm, and after the addition of Al, the preferred growth orientation for the β-Ta5Si3 coating changed from (4 0 0) to (0 0 2). The hardness, elastic modulus and contact damage resistance of the coatings were measured using a nanoindentation tester. The results showed that Al alloying improved the contact damage resistance of β-Ta5Si3 with only a slight decrease in hardness. The erosion-corrosion behavior of the two coatings was performed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing a 12 wt% concentration of silica sand under two phase slurry flow condition and in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under ultrasonic cavitation erosion conditions. This revealed that the Al alloyed β-Ta5Si3 has a higher resistance to both erosion-corrosion and ultrasonic cavitation erosion as compared to the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating.

ce:section.ce:para: Erosion–corrosion is a common material deterioration phenomenon especially for components in hydraulic equipment, such as pumps, propellers, valves, heat exchanger tubes and other equipment used in fluid handling. This promotes the premature failure of those components with decreased component lifetimes [1]. Erosion–corrosion is regarded as one of most common corrosion types in industrial applications and millions of dollars are spent every year on repairing such damage [2]. This kind of surface destruction arises from a synergistic action between mechanical erosion and electrochemical corrosion, which commonly includes two degradation modes, i.e. cavitation and liquid (or liquid + solid slurry) erosion. Owing to this synergistic effect, electrochemical corrosion and mechanical erosion become mutually reinforced, which makes the overall material loss much greater than the sum of material loss from either corrosion and erosion alone [3]. Under conditions of liquid plus solid slurry flow, slurry flow can destroy any protective passive film formed on the surface of a material and then accelerate dissolution of the underlying substrate, induced by impingement effects from the presence of solid particles. Further, any corrosion product formed under corrosive fluid flow exhibits low protective capability and is easily removed by mechanical erosion attack [4]. As another frequent damage phenomenon in hydrodynamic environments, cavitation erosion is largely due to the nucleation, growth and collapse of cavity bubbles within the flow of a liquid caused by sudden changes of internal pressure in the liquid [5,6]. The implosion of cavitation bubbles generates a large pulse of stress, accompanied by high velocity of micro-jets. The collapse in energy of the cavity exerted on solid surfaces causes severe localized plastic deformation, crack initiation, crack growth and material deterioration [7,8]. If the cavitating medium is corrosive, impacts of shock waves and micro-jets may further aggravate the degradation process, resulting from removal of the passive film formed on a material’s surface [9].

As one of the most important materials used in hydraulic systems, 316L stainless steel (SS) exhibits good corrosion resistance in many severe corrosive environments [10,11]. This is attributed to the naturally formed passive film on 316L SS surfaces, which acts as a barrier to impede corrosion in aggressive environments. Nonetheless, the passive film is easily damaged by erosion-corrosion in slurry flows, because the mechanical action induced by impact of solid particles gives rise to deformation, fracture or even delamination of passive films [12]. Hence, 316L stainless steel cannot often meet the service requirements in harsh environments, such as marine systems containing solid particles. Since the erosion–corrosion phenomenon is associated with degradation of a material surface, various surface modification techniques, including laser surface alloying [13,14], nitriding [15,16], high-velocity oxygen-fuel (HVOF) spraying [17,18], physical vapor deposition [19,20] and plasma spray or plasma transferred arc (PTA) welding [21,22], have been proposed to improve the resistance of stainless steel against erosion–corrosion damage. For example, Zhang et al. [23] investigated the cavitation erosion resistance of a FeCoCrAlNi high-entropy alloy coating synthesized on a 304 stainless steel by laser surface alloying. The results showed that the cavitation erosion resistance for this high-entropy alloy coating was ∼7.6 times that for 304 stainless steel. Similarly, Toro et al. [24] reported that corrosion–erosion resistance of a nitrided AISI 410S stainless steel tempered at 200 °C was higher than that of the uncoated steels tempered at the same temperature. However, those coatings are often not resistant to the intense and repetitive attack of solid particles and micro-jets, because such coatings exhibit low adhesion strength and readily delaminate [25]. In recent years, a range of nanocrystalline coatings, such as metallic matrix composite or transition metal silicides have been deposited onto titanium alloys and stainless steel by the double cathode glow discharge technique. Those coatings have been shown to exhibit a remarkable improvement in the surface properties of metal substrates, together with good metallurgical adhesion to the substrates [26,27].

Refractory metal silicides (RMS) have attracted significant attention for ultrahigh-temperature structural applications, due to their unique and attractive combination of physical and mechanical properties, e.g., excellent oxidation resistance, outstanding thermal stability, high melting points, attractive creep resistance and notable mechanical strength at elevated temperature [28]. Compared with other transition metal silicides, Ta5Si3 shows a greater potential for applications, where wear and corrosion resistance are critical [29]. Because of its high atomic mass and difficult processing by traditional processing methods, Ta5Si3 is better suited to serve as an anti-adhesive wear/anti-corrosion material in the form of coatings. However, because of its non-cubic structure, with low crystal symmetry and strong covalent bonding, Ta5Si3 shows inadequate room-temperature fracture toughness, which is one of the key challenges in improving its performance and potential for commercial applications. To address these issues, ternary alloying addition by substitution for both Ta and Si atoms is an attractive approach to enhance the low temperature toughness of 5:3 type metal silicides [30]. To the best of our knowledge, few studies have been paid on the cavitation erosion and erosion-corrosion performances of metal silicides.

In this work, both β-Ta5Si3 and Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coatings were deposited onto 316L SS substrates by the double cathode glow discharge technique. Erosion-corrosion tests, including under two phase slurry flow (e.g. a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution with silica sand) and ultrasonic cavitation erosion conditions (in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution), were used to evaluate the erosion-corrosion resistance of the two coatings. Weight loss analyses, together with analysis of surface morphologies of the corroded samples and electrochemical measurements, were employed to obtain an understanding of the effect of Al alloying on the erosion-corrosion performance of β-Ta5Si3 coatings.

A commercial 316L stainless steel rod with dimensions of ∼30 mm in diameter and ∼3 mm thickness was used as the substrate. The chemical composition of the 316L SS in wt% was: 0.021% C, 17.61% Cr, 12.45% Ni, 2.29% Mo, 0.69% Si, 1.05% Mn, 0.02% S, 0.031% P and the balance Fe. Before deposition, the samples were ground to a surface finish (Ra) of 0.2 μm, and subsequently the polished samples were ultrasonically cleaned in acetone and then deionized water for 10 min. Binary and ternary β-Ta5Si3 coatings were deposited on the polished 316L SS substrates using a double cathode glow discharge system. Two targets, with stoichiometric ratios of Ta50Si50 and Ta50Si50Al12.5, were prepared by sintering a mixture of Ta (99.99% purity), Si (99.99% purity) and Al (99.99% purity) powders at 900 °C for 60 min in vacuum sintering furnace. The working pressure during deposition was kept at 35 Pa with Ar gas flow rate of 80 sccm. During deposition, the bias voltage of the target and substrate electrodes were maintained at −750 V and −350 V, respectively; the distance between the target and the substrate holder was 12 mm; a deposition temperature of 700 °C was used with a deposition time of 2 h.

The crystal structures of the as-deposited coatings were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD). This was performed using a D8 ADVANCE diffractometer with a Cu-Kα (λ = 1.54056 Å) X-ray source (35 kV and 40 mA) over 2θ angles that ranged from 20° to 70° and with a step rate of 0.05° per second. To observe the microstructure of the coatings, cross-sections were prepared via standard metallographic methods and then etched using Kroll’s reagent. Cross-sections of the as-deposited coatings examined with a field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM; Hitachi, S-4800, Japan) to which an energy-dispersive X-ray analyzer (EDX-4; Philips) has been interfaced. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was performed using a JEOL JEM-2010 microscope operated at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Thin foil specimens for plan-view observation were first mechanically ground from the untreated side of the substrate and then thinned to electron transparent using a single-jet electrochemical polisher. Specimens for cross-sectional TEM analysis were prepared using a dual electron/focused ion-beam microscope (FIB, Nova Nanolab 200, FEI Company, Hillsboro, OR 97124, USA).

The elastic modulus, E, and hardness, H, of the as-deposited coatings were measured with a UMIS (Ultra-Micro Indentation System 2000, CSIRO, Sydney, Australia) workstation using a Berkovich diamond indenter. Before nanoindentation testing, initial tip calibration was employed using a standard fused silica specimen. Indentation was performed by driving the indenter at a constant loading rate of 5 mN/s with a maximum applied load of 40 mN. Values of hardness and elastic modulus were obtained using the Oliver–Pharr method [31] based on the experimentally obtained load–displacement curves. For each specimen, 20 indentation tests were performed. To examine the deformation and fracture behavior of the as-deposited coatings in greater detail, a load of 500 mN was applied to the surface of the coatings via a Berkovich diamond tip. The cross-sections of the indented areas were imaged using a dual beam FIB microscope.

The erosion-corrosion resistance of the two coatings and 316L SS under liquid–solid impingement was evaluated in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing a 12 wt% concentration of silica sand. Testing was performed using an erosion–corrosion experimental test apparatus designed by the authors, as described elsewhere [32]. The average size of sand particles used in erosion-corrosion test was 500 μm. During the erosion-corrosion process, a motor was used to drive the sample rotating in the slurry with different velocities, controlled by a speed regulating system. Three rotational speeds of 650, 850 and 1050 rpm were employed, corresponding to linear speeds of 2.38, 3.12 and 3.85 m s−1, respectively. After different erosion-corrosion test durations, the mass changes of the samples were measured using a balance with a sensitivity of 0.01 mg. For each specimen, to ensure reproducibility, the test was repeated three times, and every value reported was an average of three measurements. Under liquid–solid two phases slurry flow conditions, the total mass loss (Wt) consists of three parts: pure erosion (We), pure corrosion (Wc) and a component related to synergism between erosion and corrosion (Wec). The weight loss induced by pure erosion (We) can be determined by mass loss of the samples tested in distilled water to which sand particles has been added. The mass loss attributed to pure corrosion (Wc) was assumed to be equal to weight loss of the samples in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution without the addition of sand. The synergism between erosion and corrosion (Wec) was determined as the mass difference between Wt and the sum of We and Wc.

The cavitation erosion experiments were performed using an ultrasonic cavitation erosion rig, as described elsewhere [33]. During cavitation erosion tests, the specimen, mounted on a support table, was placed at a distance of 500 µm below the tip of the vibrating horn made from a titanium alloy. Both the horn and sample were immersed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution kept at room temperature and open to the air. Cavitation bubbles were generated at the tip of the vibrating horn, which was operated at a frequency of 20 kHz with peak-to-peak amplitude of 12 μm. The test period of each sample is 12 h and the weight loss was recorded initially and after cavitation erosion for every 2 h.

Electrochemical experiments were performed using a CHI660C electrochemical workstation with a three-electrode system. A platinum foil and a saturated calomel electrode (SCE) were used as counter and reference electrodes, respectively. Test specimens, with an exposed area of 1.0 cm2 to the solution, were used as the working electrode. Throughout this paper, all electrode potentials are referred to the SCE. The electrochemical measurements conducted in this work contain open-circuit potential (OCP) test, current responses analysis, potentiodynamic polarization test and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). In the potentiodynamic polarization tests, the specimens were polarized with a sweep rate of 1 m V/s up to 1.25 VSCE. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were performed with a sinusoidal perturbation signal with amplitude of 10 mV over a frequency range from 100 kHz to 10 mHz at each respective EOCP. Then, the data obtained from EIS was analysed and modeled through an appropriate electrical equivalent circuit (EEC) by ZSimpWin software. Furthermore, current response analysis for the samples was also conducted at a potential of 0.2 V. All these experiments were carried out at room temperature and repeated three times to ensure repeatability.

X-ray diffraction spectra recorded from the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating and Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coating are shown in Fig. 1 (a), together with the standard JCPDS data (JCPDS Card No. 09-0232) for D8m-structured β-Ta5Si3. It is evident that the diffraction peaks of the two coatings exhibit typical broadening features that originate from ultra fine grain sizes, due to the limited coherent diffracting domain size. Both XRD patterns show three diffraction peaks located at 2θ angle of 35.42°, 36.46° and 41.74°, which can be indexed to the (0 0 2), (4 0 0) and (4 1 1) planes for D8m-structured β-Ta5Si3, respectively. This suggests that the addition of Al does not cause the formation of additional crystalline phases, but rather produces a substitutional solid solution of Al-alloyed β-Ta5Si3. In contrast with the standard JCPDS data for β-Ta5Si3, the diffraction peaks for the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating were observed to shift toward lower 2θ angles, suggesting that residual compressive stresses may exist in the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating. Since the atomic radius of Al is larger than that of Si, the substitution of Al for Si in β-Ta5Si3 increases the lattice parameter, which will shift diffraction peaks towards lower angles. However, the positions of the diffraction peaks for the Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coating are nearly identical with those for the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating. This result may originate principally from a reduction in residual compressive stress after the incorporation of Al into β-Ta5Si3. Furthermore, it is worth noting that at a room temperature, β-Ta5Si3 is a thermodynamically metastable phase compared to α-Ta5Si3. Like other sputter-deposition techniques, the coatings prepared via the double cathode glow discharge process grow under non-equilibrium conditions, characterized by limited atomic assembly kinetics, including ion bombardment effect, high deposition rates and a relatively low deposition temperature [34]. This is presumably why the two coatings are composed of metastable D8m-structured β-Ta5Si3 phase. The inset table in Fig. 1(a) gives the calculated texture coefficients (TC) for different planes for the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating and Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coating. It is apparent that only the TC value of the (4 0 0) plane of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating is greater than unity, whereas the TC values of both the (4 0 0) and (0 0 2) planes for the Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coatings are larger than unity. This result indicates that the preferred growth orientation for the β-Ta5Si3 coating changes from (4 0 0) to (0 0 2) direction after the addition of Al.

Fig. 1(b) and (c) show cross-sectional SEM images, with the corresponding EDS spectra, for the binary and ternary β-Ta5Si3 coatings, respectively. From the cross-sectional SEM images, both coatings present a dense and homogeneous microstructure and are tightly adhered to their substrates, without any visible interfacial delamination or the presence of voids. EDS analysis shows that the Si/Ta or (Si + Al)/Ta atomic ratio is close to the ideal value of 0.6 for the theoretical Si/ Ta or (Si + Al)/Ta atomic ratio in β-Ta5Si3 or (Ta, Al)5Si3. This further confirms that the two coatings are composing of single phase β-Ta5Si3 or (Ta, Al)5 Si3. According to the elemental composition of the two coatings, the formula of the binary β-Ta5Si3 and Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coatings can be expressed as β-Ta5Si3 and β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3, respectively.

To obtain a detailed understanding of the microstructural characteristics of these coatings from the outer surface inwards, both cross-sectional and plan-view TEM investigations of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating were carried out, as shown in Fig. 2 . As can be seen from Fig. 2(a) and (b), the cross-sectional TEM bright-field micrographs, taken from the outer region of the coating, reveal that the coating is composed of an extremely fine, fibrous microstructure of grains aligned parallel to each other and perpendicular to the coating surface. Bright field plan-view TEM micrographs (Fig. 2(c) and (d)) indicate that the as-deposited coating is composed of spherical crystalline grains around 4 nm in diameter, separated by high-angle grain boundaries. That is, the size of these spherical nanocrystalline grains corresponds to the lateral size of the fibrous microstructure. The selected area diffraction (SAD) (inset in Fig. 2(c)) from this coating exhibits a ring pattern and the diffraction rings from inside to outside can be indexed as a D8m-structured β-Ta5Si3 (0 0 2), (4 0 0) and (4 1 1) planes, respectively, which is consistent with the data obtained from XRD. Furthermore, as shown in the bright-field HRTEM lattice image ((Fig. 2(e)), those individual spherical grains clearly display regularly spaced arrays of lattice fringes up to the grain boundaries and with spacings of 0.252 nm and 0.244 nm that correspond to the β-Ta5Si (0 0 2) and (4 0 0) planes, respectively. As can be seen from Fig. 3 (a) and (b), a sharp microstructural transformation from extremely fine grains for the coating to coarser grains for the substrate is observed. Moreover, the substrate/coating interface shows good adherence and integrity.

The typical load versus depth curves extracted from the nanoindentation test performed with a maximum load of 40 mN on the two coatings and the uncoated 316L SS substrate are shown in Fig. 4 . The maximum indenter penetration depth for the two coatings was less than 350 nm, which is less than 10% of the coating thickness. The result implies that the measured mechanical properties reflect the mechanical behavior of each of the two coatings, without any contributions from the substrate. From the load–displacement curves, the maximum indentation depth of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating is slightly greater than that of the β-Ta5Si3 coating, representing a small reduction in the resistance to local plastic deformation by Al alloying. Table 1 gives the values of the hardness and Young’s modulus for the two coatings and 316L SS. The hardness and elastic modulus values for the two coatings are significantly greater than those for uncoated 316L SS. Both elastic modulus and hardness of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating are slightly lower than that of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating, suggesting that the addition of Al decreases the hardness and elastic modulus of β-Ta5Si3.

To evaluate the effect of Al additions on the contact damage resistance of the β-Ta5Si3 coating, the surface and cross-sectional morphologies of 500 mN indents on the binary and ternary β-Ta5Si3 coatings were examined using a FIB, as shown in Fig. 5 . From the surface morphologies of the indentation impressions (shown in Fig. 5(a) and (b)), some delamination at the edge of the spherical indent, as well as ring cracks within the indent itself and radial cracks that have propagated outside of the indent area were observed for the β-Ta5Si3 coating. On the contrary, no cracks or flaking were observed either inside or outside of the spherical indent in the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating. As can be seen from Fig. 5(c) and (d), in the case of the β-Ta5Si3 coating, radial cracks were observed to propagate at an angle to the direction of applied load on the sample. However, no micro-cracking was visible within the sub-surface below the indent obtained from the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating. Careful inspection of cross-sectional TEM images for the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating reveals that the fibrous microstructure underneath the indentation area underwent significant deformation, manifested by the elongated grains being oriented perpendicular to the direction of applied load. This suggests that most of the plastic deformation caused by the normal load during indentation is accommodated by the ternary β-Ta5Si3 grains. Nonetheless, deformation of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating is limited and it retains a fine fibrous microstructure that is parallel to the normal direction of the coating surface. This indicates that the resultant deformation energy of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating is dissipated in a brittle manner, characterized by crack initiation and propagation. As grain size is reduced to tens of nanometers, dislocation image forces are enough to exclude dislocations by repelling them into the grain boundaries, leading to the absence of dislocation activity in such nanocrystalline materials [35]. Consequently, deformation is largely dominated by grain-boundary accommodation mechanisms, rather than a dislocation-mediated process. For the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating, the lower value of compressive residual stress would be more likely to activate grain boundary sliding or rotation, thus avoiding the formation of micro-cracks beneath the indentation [36]. Hence, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has a greater capacity to accommodate deformation, without crack nucleation and propagation, and thus the damage resistance of the coating is improved.

To investigate the effect of flow velocity on erosion–corrosion performance of the coatings, the dynamic electrochemical tests were carried out at flow velocities varying from 0 to 3.85 m/s as a function of exposure time, as shown in Fig. 6 . Fig. 7 presents the current responses of the two coatings and an uncoated 316L SS substrate at different flow velocities in NaCl solution with, and without, sand particles at a potential of +0.2 VSCE. As shown in Fig. 7(a), under the condition of single phase flow (3.5 wt% NaCl solution), the current density of uncoated 316L SS rises slowly within test time, indicating that the impingement of liquid flow has little influence on the current response of 316L SS within the tested flow velocities. By comparison, the current densities of the two coatings exhibit extremely low values of about 1 μA/cm2 and are almost independently of flow velocity. As shown in Fig. 7(b), in the case of liquid–solid two phase slurry flow (3.5 wt% NaCl solution with silica sand concentrations of 12 wt%), the current densities of uncoated 316L SS increase more than two orders of magnitude as the flow velocities increase from 0 to 3.85 m/s, and simultaneously, the current response curve shows a significant volatility, manifested by high level noise signal. However, for the two coatings, the current density increases only slightly with an increase of flow velocity, accompanied by only small fluctuations. Generally, the change in current transients reflects passive film removal (or the metastable pit formation) or re-passivation, namely, a rise or a drop in current density is related to passive film removal or repair processes [37]. Hence, the passive films formed on the two coatings exhibit good self-repair abilities, whereas the passive film formed on 316L SS is prone to suffer destruction resulting from the impingement of the presence of sand particles in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Whether or not single phase flow or liquid–solid two phase slurry flow, the current densities of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating is smaller than that of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating, suggesting that Al alloying is favorable to enhancing the stability of the passive film formed on a β-Ta5Si3 coating.

Fig. 8 shows the typical potentiodynamic polarization curves obtained from the two coatings and bare 316L SS measured at various flow velocities in liquid–solid slurry flow. The relevant electrochemical parameters, such as corrosion current density (icorr) and corrosion potentials (Ecorr), extracted from the polarization curves using a Tafel extrapolation method are summarized in Table 2 . As shown in Fig. 8, the potentiodynamic polarization curves of all the tested samples are simultaneously shifted to the left side and upwards with increasing flow velocities, namely, moving to a more negative potential and a larger current region. Accordingly, the corrosion current densities (icorr) increases and the corrosion potentials (Ecorr) decrease with an increase in flow velocity for all tested samples. The high slurry flow velocity not only enhances the transport of aggressive Cl− to the sample surface and impact energy of sand particles present in the flowing liquid, but also facilitates the transport of metallic ions away from the sample surface [38]. This suggests that the impinging liquid–solid flow significantly increases the corrosion rates of the tested samples compared to static conditions. Generally, the corrosion resistance of a material is proportional to the Ecorr, but inversely proportional to icorr. As can be seen from Table 2, at an identical flow velocity, the Ecorr values of the tested samples increases in the order of 316L SS < β-Ta5Si3 coating < β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating, and the i corr values of the tested sample are in the reverse order of the Ecorr values. These data clearly suggest that the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has the highest corrosion resistance under liquid–solid slurry flow. It can be seen from Fig. 8 that the polarization curves of the two coatings show a wide passive region, without observed trans-passivation zone in the anodic branch, whereas the polarization curve of 316L SS is the absence of a passive region in the anodic branch at flow velocity of 3.85 m s−1, meaning that this flow velocity aggravates the degree of its erosion–corrosion. Furthermore, under the same flow velocity, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating exhibits the lowest current densities in the passive region, followed by the β-Ta5Si3 coating, and uncoated 316L SS has the largest current densities in the passive region. This suggests that the addition of Al improves the resistance of passive film formed on the β-Ta5Si3 coating to the solid/liquid impingement.

As a versatile and sensitive technique, EIS has been widely employed to obtain a deeper understanding of protective character of different metallic and ceramic coatings under corrosive environments [39]. To further evaluate the surface damage of the tested samples after different times of slurry impingement (in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution plus 12 wt% sand particles at flow velocity of 3.85 m/s), EIS measurements were performed at their respective open-circuit potentials in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution, represented by both Nyquist, Bode magnitude and phase angle plots, as shown in Fig. 9 . It is clear that the responses of tested samples in the Nyquist complex plan display similar features, characterized by a depressed capacitive semicircle over the frequency range studied. With prolonged slurry impingement time, the semicircles for all the tested samples become more depressed and their diameters are reduced. Similarly, the low frequency limit (|Z|f0) in the Bode-magnitude plots decrease with an extension of exposure time, indicating a steady increase in accumulated damage with prolonging the solid/liquid impingement time. From the corresponding Bode phase angle plots, under static medium condition, all the tested samples show a broad plateau with a maximum phase angle approximately −90° in the middle and low frequency ranges, which is the typical feature for passive materials. With increasing the solid/liquid impingement time, the Bode phase plots of the tested samples exhibit narrower phase plateaus and smaller phase angle maximum, and the phase plateaus has split into two clear phase maxima located in the high and low frequency ranges, respectively.

To achieve a deeper insight into the electrochemical processes occurring at the interface between the tested samples and corrosive slurry flow at different exposure times, the measured impedance data was quantitatively analyzed using the appropriate electric equivalent circuit (EEC). A modified Randles circuit (Fig. 10 (a)) was employed to fit the experimental impedance data of uncoated 316L SS before the erosion-corrosion test. In this circuit (Rs(QpRp)), Rs is the solution resistance, and Qp and Rp represent the capacitive and the resistive behavior of the passive oxide film. For the two coatings before the erosion-corrosion test and the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating after the erosion-corrosion of 24 h, an equivalent circuit (Rs(Qdl(Rct(QcRc)))) (Fig. 10(b)) was adopted to model their impedance data. In the equivalent circuit model, Rs corresponds to the solution resistance between the working electrode and the reference one, Qdl is a constant-phase element (CPE) for double layer capacitance, Rct refers to the charge transfer resistance across the passive film-medium interface, and Qc and Rc are the resistance and capacitance of the passive film. The EIS data of the tested samples obtained after the erosion-corrosion test over different times in slurry flow can be fitted using the EEC consisting of two RC circuits connected in series((Rs(QpRp)(QbRb)), as shown in Fig. 10(c). In this equivalent circuit, Rs is the solution resistance, Qb and Rb represent the capacitance and resistance of the inner barrier layer, Qp and Rp represent the capacitance and resistance of the outer porous layer. This EEC indicates that the passive films formed on the samples exhibit a two-layer structure, composed of a compact inner layer and a porous outer layer. The impedance of CPE (ZCPE) is expressed as
Z
CPE
=
[
Q
(
j ω
)
n
]
- 1
, where Q is the frequency independent parameter (Ω−1 cm−2 sn), ω is the angular frequency (rad s−1), j is the imaginary number and n is CPE power with values between 1.0 and 0, related to a slope of log |Z| vs. log f [40]. Depending on n values, the CPE describes an ideal capacitor (n = 1), a Warburg impedance (n = 0.5), or an ideal resistor (n = 0). As can be seen from the Bode plots (Fig. 9), the experimental (data in points) and simulated (solid line) data were consistent and the Chi-square values (χ2) are each of the order of 10−3–10−4, signifying that the three proposed EEC reflect reasonably the electrochemical process operating in the test samples.

As shown in Table 3 , under the condition of a static medium, the resistance values of Rc for the two coatings are of the order of 107 Ωcm2, which are two orders of magnitude greater than that of uncoated 316L SS. After impingement over different times by the slurry flow, both the resistance values for the inner barrier layer (Rb) and the resistance of the outer porous layer (Rp) are progressively reduced for the tested samples, implying that the passive films formed on the samples have gradually lost their protectiveness. For each sample, the resistance values of Rb are 2–4 orders of magnitude bigger than that of the Rp, suggesting that corrosive species are much easier to penetrate to the outer porous layer and, thus, the corrosion resistance of the samples are highly dependent on the inner barrier layer. At the same exposure time, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has a higher Rb value than the β-Ta5Si3 coating, and the Rb values of the two coatings are 1–2 orders of magnitude greater than that of uncoated 316L SS. The results show that both coatings can enhance the erosion–corrosion resistance of 316L SS and Al alloying can further improve this behavior for the β-Ta5Si3 coating. Usually, the n value can be used as an indicator to describe the surface roughening of an electrode, which is often triggered by chemical attack and mechanical impact during the erosion–corrosion test using slurry [41]. In other words, the smoother the electrode is, the higher the value of n becomes. As expected, the nb values for each sample are obviously larger than the np values, and both of the nb and np values decrease with an increase in exposure time. It can be seen from Table 3 that the nb and np values for the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating are greater than those for the other two samples, confirming that the passive films formed on the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating preserve better integrity with a lower porosity. Therefore, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating suffers less damage induced by mechanical and electrochemical attack.

During the test, samples are exposed to a liquid–solid two phase slurry flow. As such, the total weight loss (Wt) of the samples comes from the following three parts: a pure mechanical damage component (We), a pure electrochemical deterioration component and the synergy effect component between them (Ws) [42]. Thus, the total weight loss rate in slurry flow could be described according to the following equation:
(1)
W t
=
W e
+
W c
+
W s

Fig. 11 (a) presents the components of weight loss for the test materials after erosion-corrosion testing was performed in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution containing 12 wt% sand particles at flow velocity of 3.85 m/s. From Fig. 11(a), it is apparent that the total weight loss rate, pure electrochemical corrosion rate, pure mechanical damage rate and synergistic weight loss rate for the 316L SS substrate are more than twice as much as those for the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating and the β-Ta5Si3 coating, indicating that the mass loss of the uncoated substrate by slurry impingement was substantially reduced by coating with either the binary or ternary β-Ta5Si3 coatings. As shown in Fig. 11(b), the 316L SS has the highest relative synergistic weight loss rate in the total weight loss rate among the three tested samples, suggesting that the material removal is predominantly controlled by synergistic interactions between erosion and corrosion. On the contrary, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating exhibits the lowest relative synergistic weight loss rate, suggesting that the addition of Al to β-Ta5Si3 enhances the resistance to erosion–corrosion caused by slurry impingement.

Fig. 12 shows typical SEM images of surface morphologies for the tested samples before and after 20 h of exposure to liquid–solid two phase slurry flow at a flow velocity of 3.85 m/s. From Fig. 12(b), the uncoated 316L SS exhibits characteristic surface morphologies induced by a combined attack of a corrosive media and abrasive particles, reflected by the appearance of a large number of shallow craters, deep grooves, plastic deformation and corrosion pits. Consistent with the current response curves (Fig. 7) and potentiodynamic polarization curves (Fig. 8), the uncoated 316L SS exhibits active-dissolution behavior at a flow velocity of 3.85 m s−1. This is presumably due to the continuous removal of the naturally formed surface passive film by the relative movement of the corrosive medium containing sand particles [43]. Moreover, the lower hardness of the uncoated 316L SS compared with that of sand particles in suspension, makes it particularly susceptible to cutting or deformation by repeated erosion impacts of solid particles. As shown in Fig. 12(d) and (e), both coatings display a flat and largely featureless appearance with no evidence of plastic deformation and corrosion pits, thus providing good protection for the 316L SS substrate from slurry impingement. From Figs. 7 and 8, the two coatings still retain passivity under the employed experimental condition. This indicates that when removal of passive films occurs, the two coatings exhibit improved levels of self-repair to re-passivate them again. The high hardness of the two coatings, endowed by strong covalent-dominated atomic bonds in their crystal structures, can not only withstand micro-cutting or micro-ploughing exerted by sand particles, alleviating surface plastic deformation, but also provide an effective mechanical support to the passive film, making it more difficult to be removed. Note that some small craters, as indicated by arrows, are observed on the eroded surface of the β-Ta5Si3 coating. Owing to its low ductility, the impact of sand particles generates high tensile stresses on the surface of the β-Ta5Si3 coating, leading to a small number of localized fractures and local detachment. Therefore, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating, with higher toughness, affords more effective to protect the 316L SS substrate than the β-Ta5Si3 coating under slurry impingement.

The influences of Al alloying on the ultrasonic cavitation erosion behavior of the β-Ta5Si3 coating in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution were investigated using a magnetostrictive-induced ultrasonic cavitation erosion at different electrical input powers (0, 600 and 800 W). The electrical input powers in open circuit potential (EOCP) and current response measurements varied with exposure time in a manner as shown in Fig. 13 (a). Fig. 13(b) displays the EOCP of the two coatings and 316L SS under different ultrasonic cavitation conditions in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Clearly, when the electrical input powers increased from 0 to 600 W, the EOCP for 316L SS rapidly shifted in positive directions, and then gradually decreased. With a further increase in the electrical input power, the EOCP still constantly declined with progressively increasing fluctuation. An initial rise in EOCP may be interpreted as the increase in the mass transfer of oxygen due to the formation and collapse of cavitation bubbles induced by the vibration horn [44]. Subsequently, the EOCP moves to more negative values, representing a thinning or localized destruction of the passive film formed on 316L SS caused by repetitive impacts of the collapsing bubble [45]. However, over the cavitation period, the reduction in EOCP for the two coatings was negligible in comparison with 316L SS, indicating that 316L SS is more vulnerable to the active dissolution reaction driven by turbulent flow through cavity bubbles than the two coatings. Fig. 13(c) presents the current densities of the tested samples as a function of the electrical input powers at a constant potential of +0.2 VSCE in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. When the ultrasonic cavitation was turned on, the current density of 316L SS sharply increased and its value increased significantly with an increase in electrical input power, accompanied by wide current fluctuations. In contrast, under ultrasonic cavitation conditions, the current densities of the two coatings exhibited only a very slight change with small current fluctuations, especially for the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating. A sharp increase in the current density is mainly attributed to continuous breakdown of the passive film, supply of oxygen through cavitation bubbles and presence of Cl- ions in the medium. Current fluctuations occur as a consequence on depassivation and repassivation events on the surface of the tested samples. The electrical input power is proportional to the transmitted power and impact pressure of cavities. With increasing electrical input power, the impacts of shock waves and micro-jets become more intense, thus accelerating the depassivation process. From the results of EOCP and current response shown in Fig. 13, cavitation had the least influence on the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating. Hence, the formation of passive film grown on the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has higher electrochemical stability to suppress the active dissolution reaction and implosion of cavitation bubbles.

Fig. 14 shows typical potentiodynamic polarization curves recorded from the two coatings and uncoated 316L SS in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under ultrasonic cavitation erosion condition for a range of electrical input powers. The extracted corrosion potential (Ecorr) and corrosion current density (icorr) were summarized in Table 4 . It is apparent that all the tested samples exhibit self-passivating behavior, without the occurrence of active/passive transient peak under both static state and ultrasonic cavitation conditions. All the tested samples appear to be kinetically more active under ultrasonic cavitation conditions than under static state conditions, reflected by the measured polarization curves moving toward a more active region of a lower Ecorr and a larger icorr. Such a phenomenon has also been observed for other metals in saline water [46]. Evidently, the ultrasonic cavitation erosion exerts a negative impact on the electrochemical polarization behavior of the tested samples in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The influence of ultrasonic cavitation erosion on the polarization features of the tested samples include the following two major aspects: (1) the impact pressure of cavities and micro-jets promote mechanical damage, leading to the displacement of Ecorr towards more cathodic potentials so enhancing icorr; (2) the increase in oxygen transport driving from the implosion of cavitation bubbles results in a noble shift in Ecorr. The negative shift in Ecorr suggests that the mechanical damage is the decisive factor for the tested samples during these ultrasonic cavitation tests [47]. For 316L SS, icorr values at electrical input power values of 600 and 800 W are two and three orders of magnitude higher than for the static case, respectively, and the passive region in the anodic branch becomes narrower with an increase in input powers. In the case of the two coatings, no trans-passivation phenomenon was observed on the anodic branches of the polarization curves. Both exhibit a nobler Ecorr and much lower icorr than the uncoated 316L SS at the same electrical input power values, indicating that both coatings can effectively inhibit the cavitation erosion of 316L SS in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. Compared with the β-Ta5Si3 coating, the icorr values of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating is decreased by an order of magnification under same ultrasonic cavitation condition, revealing that the incorporation of Al can further improve the cavitation erosion resistance of β-Ta5Si3.

Fig. 15 presents representative Nyquist and Bode plots collected from the two coatings and uncoated 316L SS in a 3.5% NaCl solution after different intervals of ultrasonic cavitation erosion with the input power of 800 W. Clearly, the Nyquist plots of all tested samples present a depressed capacitive semicircle over the entire frequency range used, and the corresponding Bode-phase plots exhibit a broad plateau of a phase angle over the middle and low frequency ranges with a linear dependency between log |Z| and log f. These responses of capacitive behavior show features typical of passive behavior. With increasing exposure time for ultrasonic cavitation erosion, the diameter of the capacitive semicircles and the phase angle maximum decrease continuously. Typically, the diameter of the capacitive semicircle varies inversely with corrosion rate of a material. Thus, ultrasonic cavitation weakens corrosion resistance of the tested samples, which accords with the results of potentiodynamic polarization tests. This is closely related to the increased impact pressure of the microjets, associated chemical reaction and the transport of oxygen, resulting in a decrease in charge transfer resistance [48].

To gain a quantitative evaluation of the effect of ultrasonic cavitation on the electrochemical properties of the tested sample, the equivalent electron circuit (EEC) was used to model the data as shown in Fig. 15. The proposed equivalent electron circuits (EEC) are the same as those shown in Fig. 10 and the analyzed results are summarized in Table 5 . It is clearly seen from Table 5 that with increased exposure time for cavitation erosion, the values of Rp and Rb decrease and the corresponding values of Qp and Qb increase. This denotes a degradation of both the outer porous layer as well as the inner barrier layers with increasing cavitation erosion time. The CPE power, n value reflects parameters such as porosity, local heterogeneity and roughness of the sample surface [49]. For all the samples analysed, the np and nb values decrease with increasing cavitation erosion time, thus suggesting a steady increase in the roughness of sample surface induced by the ultrasonic cavitation. For a given ultrasonic cavitation time, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating exhibits higher values of resistance and n as compared to the binary coating, implying that Al alloying improves the compactness and protection of the passive film formed on the β-Ta5Si3 coating in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution.

Fig. 16 shows the weight loss as a function of exposure time for the two coatings and the reference 316L SS substrate at an input power of 800 W in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The reference 316L SS exhibits a typical incubation period for the first 4 h, characterized by a slow increase in weight loss rate. After 4 h the weight loss starts to rise quickly with increasing exposure time. A similar phenomenon has also been observed by other researchers [50]. In contrast, the weight loss rates for both the binary and ternary β-Ta5Si3 coatings are quite insensitive to exposure time, showing a negligible change in the weight loss with increasing exposure time. After 12 h of testing, the weight loss values of the β-Ta5Si3 coating and the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating are about 1/13 and 1/20 that of 316L SS, respectively. From the perspective of mechanical behaviour, hardness has been conventionally considered as the dominant parameter controlling cavitation erosion resistance, and the hardness of a material is proportional to its cavitation erosion resistance [51]. Taillon et al. [52] compared the cavitation erosion rate of various HVOF coatings on martensitic and ferritic stainless steels. However, they found that higher hardness correlated with better cavitation erosion resistance for steels, but for the coatings, cavitation erosion resistance of coatings was not associated with H, E, porosity or matrix fraction. The growth and collapse of bubbles is known to liberate large amounts of energy, which impacts intensively on the sample surface [53]. Hence, high toughness is essential for a cavitation erosion resistant material, since high toughness is favorable for the absorption energy resulting from the implosion of cavitation bubbles, thus mitigating the extent of any mechanical damage. In our case, although the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has a lower hardness, it exhibits a higher toughness and resistance to cavitation erosion, as compared to the β-Ta5Si3 coating. This indicates that toughness is more important than hardness in controlling cavitation erosion resistance of the β-Ta5Si3 coating. Further, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has better corrosion resistance than the β-Ta5Si3 coating, which may also assist in alleviating the synergistic effects of mechanical erosion and corrosion.

Fig. 17 shows the eroded surface morphologies of the two coatings and the reference 316L SS after 12 h of cavitation erosion-corrosion tests at an input power of 800 W in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. As shown in Fig. 17(a), severe anodic dissolution and micro-plastic deformation damage patterns, such as deep cracks and large craters surrounded by shear lips, were evident on the eroded surface of 316L SS substrate. Owing to its low mechanical strength, the impacts of microjets and shock waves render severe plastic deformation occurred on the surface of the substrate, resulting in the formation of craters and brittle cracks and material removal. Such mechanical erosion roughens the substrate and disrupts the protective passive film, thereby greatly aggravating electrochemical dissolution reaction processes in an aggressive electrolyte. In contrast, as shown in Fig. 17(b) and (c), the surface damage of the two coatings is significantly suppressed, especially for the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating. The β-Ta5Si3 coating remains essentially intact with a smooth eroded surface, except some shallow crater-like damage. In contrast, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating exhibits a smoother appearance, without any evidence of local plastic flow or corrosion pitting. Therefore, the addition of Al to β-Ta5Si3 is an effective strategy to inhibit the synergistic effects between the cavitation-induced erosion and corrosion.

To improve the erosion-corrosion resistance of 316L SS, a binary β-Ta5Si3 coating and a ternary Al-containing β-Ta5Si3 coating, both with dense and homogeneous microstructures, were fabricated onto 316L SS substrates. The mechanical properties and contact damage resistance of the two coatings were determined by nanoindentation techniques. To explore the critical role of Al alloying in regulating the erosion-corrosion behavior of the β-Ta5Si3 coating, erosion-corrosion tests, under two phase slurry flow and ultrasonic cavitation erosion conditions, were undertaken, in conjunction with an array of complementary electrochemical techniques. As compared to the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating showed a higher contact damage resistance, with a negligible decrease in hardness. Under solid/liquid slurry flow conditions, the potentiostatic tests shows that the current density of the two coatings increased slightly with the increase of flow velocities, accompanied by small fluctuations and the current densities of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating were smaller than that of the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating. Potentiodynamic polarization tests indicate that compared with the binary β-Ta5Si3 coating, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has a higher Ecorr and lower icorr; EIS tests suggest that at the same exposure time, the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has a higher Rb value than the β-Ta5Si3 coating, and the Rb values of both coatings are 1–2 orders of magnitude greater than that of uncoated 316L SS. Under ultrasonic cavitation erosion conditions, the potentiodynamic polarization tests indicate that compared with the β-Ta5Si3 coating, the icorr values of the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating are decreased by an order of magnification under same ultrasonic cavitation condition. EIS tests suggest that the β-Ta5(Si0.83Al0.17)3 coating has higher values of resistance and n, as compared to the binary coating, implying that Al alloying improves the compactness and protection of the passive film formed on the β-Ta5Si3 coating in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. The addition of Al is an effective way to improve the erosion-corrosion resistance of β-Ta5Si3 coatings through inhibiting the synergistic effect between mechanical erosion and electrochemical corrosion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support of the General Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51675267, the Key Program of the National Natural Science Foundation of China under Grant No. 51635004 and the Australian Research Council Discovery Project scheme.
